coredata.dc:title: Deep eutectic solvent-based magnetic molecular nanomaterials coupled with fluorescent carbon dots as a new strategy to highly enrich and sensitively detect doxycycline in food matrices

coredata.dc:description: The highly selective enrichment and sensitive detection of antibiotics in food are crucial for human health, environmental monitoring, and management. In this study, deep eutectic solvents (DESs) were used as eco–friendly functional monomers, a magnetic molecular nanomaterial (DES–MMIP) coupled with carbon dots (CDs) was developed as a sensitive fluorescent probe for the selective enrichment and detection of trace amounts of doxycycline (DOX). Under optimal conditions, the method exhibited good linearity within 0.5–50 μg·L−1, a low detection limit of 0.34 nM for DOX detection. Furthermore, the DES–MMIP displayed excellent reusability and storage stability. With responsive, excellent selectivity and an eco–friendly nature, the established method is successful in detecting DOX in food matrices, with an average spiked recovery of 87.5 %–97.8 %. This strategy of integrating CDs and DES–MMIP for DOX detection conforms to the green concept of environmental protection and provides a new material and research approach for efficient analysis of DOX in complex matrices.

head.ce:title: Deep eutectic solvent-based magnetic molecular nanomaterials coupled with fluorescent carbon dots as a new strategy to highly enrich and sensitively detect doxycycline in food matrices

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The highly selective enrichment and sensitive detection of antibiotics in food are crucial for human health, environmental monitoring, and management. In this study, deep eutectic solvents (DESs) were used as eco–friendly functional monomers, a magnetic molecular nanomaterial (DES–MMIP) coupled with carbon dots (CDs) was developed as a sensitive fluorescent probe for the selective enrichment and detection of trace amounts of doxycycline (DOX). Under optimal conditions, the method exhibited good linearity within 0.5–50 μg·L−1, a low detection limit of 0.34 nM for DOX detection. Furthermore, the DES–MMIP displayed excellent reusability and storage stability. With responsive, excellent selectivity and an eco–friendly nature, the established method is successful in detecting DOX in food matrices, with an average spiked recovery of 87.5 %–97.8 %. This strategy of integrating CDs and DES–MMIP for DOX detection conforms to the green concept of environmental protection and provides a new material and research approach for efficient analysis of DOX in complex matrices.

ce:keyword.ce:text: histidine and serine functionalized graphene quantum dots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are one of the most effective drugs in the treatment of diseases, but the widespread abuse of antibiotics in the the livestock and aquaculture industries has led to increasingly serious livestock products safety issues (Gil, 2023; Rui, 2023). Doxycycline is one of the most commonly used antibiotics in the livestock and aquaculture industries because of its high oral absorption rate and excellent antibacterial effect. DOX consists of a tetracyclic ring system, consisting of two benzene rings and two heterocycles. The ring system is connected to various functional groups, including hydroxyl, methyl, amino, and carbonyl groups, forming multiple chiral centers (Zaidi et al., 2019). However, the long–term consumption of food containing DOX residues can disrupt the normal balance of microbial communities in the human body and damage digestive system, blood system, liver and kidney function. Especially, in children, it will lead to obesity, increased risk of allergies, tooth discoloration or skeletal dysplasia (Manikandan et al., 2020; Sadighara et al., 2023). Therefore, DOX is prohibited for pregnant women, lactating women, and children under 8 years of age. In addition, low-dose or irregular use of DOX can weaken their bactericidal effect, providing more space for bacteria to survive and mutate, leading to bacterial resistance (Karine et al., 2022). European Union, China and Japan have regulated residue limits of DOX in poultry and fish (Chen et al., 2021). At present, it is urgent and important to establish a quicker and more accurate method to monitor the residue of DOX in livestock and aquaculture food and control its illegal abuse. In this study, we used DOX as the research object, aiming to develop new technologies and detection methods with high selectivity, simplified extraction, separation, and enrichment.

With the development of material chemistry, molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) and deep eutectic solvents (DESs) are gradually being used for the extraction and separation of trace substance. However, there are few reports on the synthesis of MIPs using DES system as a functional monomer for DOX adsorption and recognition, especially in terms of stimulus responsiveness. MIPs prepared by traditional methods often suffer from incomplete removal of template molecules, disruption of recognition sites, and significant consumption of organic solvents, etc. (BelBruno, 2019; Becskereki et al., 2021; Marć et al., 2024). At present, preparation of eco–friendly magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers (Eco–MMIPs) to realize controlled adsorption or release of target compounds has become a new research topic in molecular imprinting technology (MIT). In this research, we overcame the shortcomings of traditional imprinting polymers article using surface imprinting technology to fix the molecular imprinting layer to form magnetic nanoparticle surface. At the same time, DESs as a green solvent have been introduced into the preparation of MIPs because of the simple preparation, cost–effective, low toxicity and highly biodegradable characteristics, which can strongly interact with solutes (Nahar & Thickett, 2021; Liu et al., 2016). Moreover, carbon dots (CDs) – a novel fluorescent material with excellent properties including the optical properties, biocompatibility, and low toxicity was innovatively adopted to establish MMIP@CDs Sensors to realize high sensitivity extraction and detection. Before commencing the experiment, quantum mechanics simulations were utilized to investigate the molecular dynamics of DESs in MIPs systems and the interactions of compounds, also to predict and explain the specificity observed in the experiment. The density functional theory (DFT) method, which features high accuracy and low computation, was adopted to precisely calculate and simulate the intra– or inter–molecular interactions (Medvedev et al., 2017). Then, we successfully predicted the most stable configuration of DES and synthesized Eco–MMIPs using DES system as functional monomer, ethylene-modified Fe3O4@SiO2–CH=CH2 as a magnetic carrier, carbon dots (CDs) as a sensitive fluorescent probe to selectively enrich and detect trace amounts of DOX in food samples.

The synthesized materials were comprehensively characterized via Fourier–transform infrared spectroscopy (FT–IR), X–ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), and vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM). In addition, the adsorption performance and selective recognition ability of Eco–MMIPs were further evaluated through adsorption kinetics and isothermal adsorption studies. This study also conducted specific adsorption and quantitative analysis of DOX in real samples via MSPE to validate the reliability of the method. This new environment–friendly research approach with the great advantages of fast magnetic separation, high selectivity, high adsorption capacity and reusability, not only accurately extracted DOX from complex food matrices, but also laid the foundation to detect other types of abused antibiotics in complex matrices.

Doxycycline hydrochloride, Oxytetracycline (OTC), Chlortetracycline (CTC), Minocycline (MC), Tetracycline (TC), and choline chloride (ChCl) were obtained from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 3–Methacryloxypropyltrimethoxysilane (KH570), ethylene methacrylate (EGDMA), methacrylic acid (MAA), Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA–2Na) and 2,2′–Azobis(2–methylpropionitrile) (AIBN) were procured from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethyl acetate, ethanol, hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), urea, citric acid (CA), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), ammonia solution (NH4·OH, 25 %–28 %), Iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), Iron chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O), ammonium acetate, triethylamine and N–hexane were acquired from Tianjin Kaitong Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Acetic acid (AA) was provided by Zesheng Technology Co., Ltd. (Anhui, China). Methanol (MeOH), acetonitrile (ACN) were bought from Merck Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

The theoretical optimization of the compounds was based on the literature (Lao & Sun, 2013). Using Gaussian16.0, GaussView6.0, and DFT, a small basis set (B3LYP/6–31 + G(d,p)) was chosen for geometry optimization and vibrational analysis, whereas a large basis set (B3LYP/def2TZVP) was selected for calculating the single–point energies of the compounds to obtain optimal conformations without imaginary frequencies (Wang et al., 2023). The DES is optimized on the basis of the results of the electrostatic potential diagrams of each compound without symmetry constraints. The equations for calculating the Gibbs free energy ( ∆ G ) are as follows:
(1)
∆ G =
G Complex
−
G ChCl
− n
G MAA

The DES was synthesized by mixing ChCl and MAA at a molar ratio of 1:2 and heating to 80 °C, after which the mixture was mixed well until a clear and transparent solution was obtained to form stable DES.

Using the hydrothermal method, urea and CA were ultrasonically dissolved in a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) reactor containing 20 mL of ultrapure water and reacted at 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling to 25 °C, the supernatant was collected by filtering with a 0.22 μm filter membrane after centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. The filtrate was subjected to dialysis for 24 h in dialysis tubing (1000 Da). Finally, the dialysis fluid was freeze–dried to obtain CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and modification of Fe3O4 magnetic particles
ce:section.ce:para: First, a coprecipitation method was adopted to synthesize magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Mahmud et al., 2020). In brief, FeCl2·4H2O and Fe3Cl·6H2O (1:2, molar ratio) were ultrasonically dissolved in a 500 mL flask containing 200 mL of distilled water, and 30 mL of NH4·OH was added at once, forming a black precipitate immediately. The black solid product was separated using an external magnet after vigorous stirring for 30 min at 80 °C with nitrogen protection. Finally, the particles were cleaned with water and ethanol to neutrality and dried at 50 °C in a vacuum to obtain Fe3O4 nanoparticles.

Two silane coupling agents were used to modify Fe3O4 in two steps via the sol–gel method (Liang et al., 2019). 1 g of magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles were ultrasonically dispersed in 250 mL of ethanol–water, followed by the addition of 4 mL of NH4·OH and 3 mL of TEOS in batches with vigorous stirring for 8 h at 25 °C. To obtain Fe3O4@SiO2, the mixture was cleaned three to five times with water and ethanol and dried at 50 °C under vacuum. After that, 1 g of magnetic Fe3O4@SiO2 particles and 20 mL of KH570 were added to 400 mL of ethanol with vigorous stirring for 8 h at 70 °C with nitrogen protection. The product was washed three times with water and ethanol and dried at 50 °C under vacuum to obtain magnetic Fe3O4@SiO2–CH=CH2 nanoparticles.

MIPs were prepared via surface imprinting. In brief, 0.5 mmol of DOX was completely dissolved in 50 mL of MeOH, followed by the addition of 350 μL of DES, mixed evenly and self–assembled for 3 h. Subsequently, 200 mg of Fe3O4@SiO2–CH=CH2 was added, and prepolymerization was continued for 9 h. After that, 20 mmol EGDMA and 50 mg AIBN were added and sonicated for 15 min. Then, the polymerization was carried out at 60 °C for 12 h in a sealed system by nitrogen protection.
The preparation steps for DES–MNIP were identical to those for DES–MMIP except that no template was added.

DES–MMIP material was added to the DOX–containing sample mixture and oscillated at 25 °C for 90 min for DOX enrichment, and the magnetic DES–MMIP was separated by an external magnet. The DOX in the adsorbent was subsequently eluted with 8 mL of MeOH:AA (V/V, 9:1) (2 times, 6 min each). After the eluent was swept with N2 until dry, the residue was redissolved in 50 μL of MeOH, buffer (940 μL, pH 10), and CDs solution dissolved in ultra–pure water (10 μL, 7.5 mg·mL−1). After incubation for 4 min, the fluorescence response of the final solution was obtained with an excitation wavelength of 320 nm and an emission wavelength of 425 nm.

Adsorption kinetics and adsorption isotherm experiments for DOX adsorption by DES–MMIP (DES–MNIP) were carried out under optimal conditions. For adsorption kinetics, 45 mg of DES–MMIP (DES–MNIP) was dispersed in 40 mL of a DOX aqueous solution and oscillated at 25 °C. The concentration of DOX was determined at different intervals (5–180 min), and the adsorption of DES–MMIP (DES–MNIP) under different contact time conditions was calculated. The adsorption capacity for DOX and imprinting factors were calculated via the following equations (Febrianto et al., 2009; Nantasenamat et al., 2007):

where Q (μg·g−1), QDES–MMIP and QDES–MNIP represent the adsorbed amount, and C0 (μg·L−1) and Ce (μg·L−1) represent the initial and equilibrium concentrations of DOX, respectively. In addition, C0 and Ce are from the analyte standard curve. V (mL) represents the total volume of the DOX solution, and M (mg) represents the mass of DES–MMIP (DES–MNIP) added.

To evaluate the adsorption isotherms, 45 mg of DES–MMIP (DES–MNIP) was added to 40 mL of a DOX solution at different initial concentrations (10–240 μg·L−1) and oscillated at 25 °C for 90 min. After reaching adsorption equilibrium, the nanoparticles were separated by an external magnet, and the concentration of DOX was determined to calculate the amount of DOX captured by the polymer.

The ability of DES–MMIP (DES–MNIP) to recognize DOX was investigated via selective adsorption experiments. The structural analogs OTC, CTC, MC and TC were selected as emulative substances to assess the selective adsorption of DOX by DES–MMIP (DES–MNIP). 45 mg of DES–MMIP (DES–MNIP) was dispersed in 40 mL of a mixed solution of DOX and other interferences, in which DOX concentration was kept at 30 μg·L−1, and the concentration of interferences was also all at 30 μg·L−1.The MSPE process and fluorescence detection process were performed as the same as those in Section 2.8. All experiments were carried out under optimal experimental conditions. Then, the concentration of each substance was calculated and the adsorption amount was obtained. The specific recognition ability and the selectivity of recognition of the DES-MMIP (DES-MNIP) can be evaluated by imprinting factor (α) and separation factor (β), respectively (Shen et al., 2022).

where QDES-MMIP (QDES-MNIP) means the adsorption capacity of DOX on QDES-MMIP (QDES-MNIP). αDOX is the imprinting factor for GA and αother is the imprinting factor for other structural analogues.

To investigate the recyclability of the prepared adsorbent, the same batch of DES–MMIP was dispersed in a DOX solution at a concentration of 30 μg·L−1 several times, and the concentration of DOX was detected after 90 min of adsorption. The template was subsequently completely removed from the adsorbent with an eluent, dried and collected for subsequent cyclic adsorption experiments. Seven cycles of adsorption resolution were set up.

To verify the practical application value of the established method, we selected commercially available milk, fish, pork, beef and eggs (Heilongjiang, China) as test samples. Various types of extracts were acquired through pretreatment and filtration with a 0.22 μm filter membrane, followed by MSPE and measurement of the fluorescence intensity of the samples, with the recovery rates being calculated. Verification experiments are usually conducted through nationally prescribed standard methods to verify the practicality of synthetic materials and the accuracy and reliability of established detection methods. In this experiment, high–performance liquid chromatography–ultraviolet detector (HPLC–UV) was used to detect the content of DOX in food samples, and qualitative analysis was performed according to the retention time of the chromatographic peak, quantitative analysis was performed according to the peak area, and the recovery rate was calculated. The chromatographic column was Agilent TC – C18 (4.6 mm × 250 mm, 5 μm).Acetate buffer (0.25 mol·L−1 ammonium acetate solution −0.1 mol·L−1 ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA–2Na) solution – triethylamine (100:10:1, V: V: V), adjusting pH to 8.8 by AA) – acetonitrile (85:15) was used as mobile phase. The flow rate was 1.0 mL·min−1. The column temperature was 35 °C. The detection wavelength was 280 nm. The injection volume was 20 μL. (Information on the pretreatment samples is provided in the supplementary material).

50 μL of methanol standard solution of DOX at different concentrations, buffer (940 μL, pH 10), and CDs solution dissolved in ultra–pure water (10 μL, 7.5 mg·mL−1) were accurately measured in test tubes and configured as a series of standard solution concentrations (0.5–50 μg·mL−1). Under optimal conditions, wavelength scanning was performed at excitation and emission wavelengths fixed at 320 nm and 425 nm, respectively, to produce the fluorescence emission spectra of the standard samples. The limit of detection (LOD) was explored by recording the FL intensity of CDs with blank samples after MSPE pretreatment for 15 times. The LOD was calculated according to the formula of LOD = 3σ/s (where σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample (n = 15) and s is the slope of the linear range line). In addition, DOX solutions with concentrations of 1 μg·L−1, 5 μg·L−1 and 10 μg·L−1 were prepared and detected and analyzed 6 times a day to evaluate intraday precision. For the analytical samples at the above level, measurements were taken once a day for 6 consecutive days to evaluate the interday precision.

To obtain the most stable configuration of the molecule, we used DFT theory to optimize the initial structure of the compound without imaginary frequencies, obtaining the molecular structure with the smallest single point energy and the most stable configuration. We found that the configuration is most stable when chloride ions form hydrogen bonds with hydroxyl groups and –CH in choline cations (Fig. S1, Table S1). In addition, to clarify the optimal conformation of the complex, we analyzed the hydrogen bonding sites through the NBO charge distribution and molecular electrostatic potential under the optimal configuration. The darker the red color in the charge distribution graph is, the more electronegative it is, indicating that the atom is vulnerable to attack by electrophilic reagents and thus loses electrons. In contrast, the darker the blue color is, the more electropositive the atom is, indicating that atoms are highly susceptible to attack by nucleophilic reagents (Mehmood et al., 2018). It was apparent that the proton donor of MAA is H12 (0.505), and the proton acceptor is O2 (−0.603). The electrostatic potential of the choline cation in ChCl was positive, and that of Cl− was negative. The van der Waals surface penetration of the two was obvious, reflecting the complementary character of the electrostatic potentials and the nature of the electrostatic attraction interaction of hydrogen bonding, suggesting that Cl− ions are more inclined to form multiple stable hydrogen bonds with cations (Fig. S2). On this basis, we constructed models of the DES complex and calculated their Gibbs free energy. The formation of all the complexes was a spontaneous process and was more prone to spontaneous polymerization in the DES1 configuration, which is consistent with the results of the literature (Fig. 1 , Table S2) (Li & Row, 2020).

Functional monomers are crucial for the synthesis of MIPs. To maximize the synergistic effect of MAA and ChCl, the effect of the DES volume on the molecular imprinting efficiency was investigated. DES–MMIP was synthesized by fixing the molar quantity of the template, magnetic carrier, crosslinker and initiator and adding different volumes of DES to study the effects of DES on the adsorption properties. Interestingly, we found that if vigorous stirring is not performed during the preparation of the imprinted polymers, the products may form a gel–like state, complicating separation. This may be due to the fact that the polymer synthesis temperature is 60 °C, close to the boiling point of MeOH (64.00 to 65.00 °C, 760.00 mmHg), with a long synthesis time (12h), resulting in complete evaporation of MeOH in an open system and synthesis failure. However, under the closed system, the product presents two states (normal suspension and abnormal gel), which may be attributed to the the lack of stirring or insufficient stirring during the polymerization process, resulting in the formation of a gel-like products. Therefore, the polymerization reaction should be continuously stirred in a water bath to obtain uniform monodisperse magnetic particles. The results revealed that as the volume of functional monomers increased, the adsorption capacity of DES–MMIP gradually increased. At 350 μL, the polymer reached the optimal adsorption effect, and the molecular imprinting efficiency was the best. The imprinting efficiency subsequently decreased as the volume of the functional monomer increased (Fig. 2 ). The former is attributed to the lack of specific binding sites provided, resulting in the poor recognition of the target compound by the imprinted polymer. The latter is due to the presence of too many functional monomers, which causes the imprinting layer to be too thick and results in an embedding phenomenon, leading to excessive steric hindrance.

The morphology, size, and lattice of the carbon dots were observed via TEM. The synthesized CDs had a relatively uniform particle size of approximately 3–5 nm, and upon magnification, the characteristic lattice structure clearly exhibited graphite lattice stripes (Fig. 3a). SEM results showed Fe3O4 particles with a regular spherical structure and relatively uniform particle size (Fig. 3b). After being encapsulated by SiO2, the magnetic dipole attraction between magnetic nanospheres was reduced, improving their dispersibility. However, surface–modified KH570 caused aggregation between nanospheres (Fig. 3c). Both DES–MMIP (Fig. 3d) and DES–MNIP (Fig. 3e) exhibited aggregation phenomena, with the interparticle gaps in DES–MMIP being wider compared to DES–MNIP. This may be attributed to the presence of template molecules in DES–MMIP, which form specific pores during the imprinting process, allowing the template molecules to be specifically adsorbed.

FT–IR can be used to identify the functional groups of synthesized materials. The infrared spectrum of the CDs showed that the broad absorption peaks in the range of 3600–2500 cm−1 were derived from the stretching vibrations of OH and NH functional groups, which contributed to the hydrophilicity of the CDs to a certain extent. The characteristic peak at 1702 cm−1 is attributed to the carboxyl group, whereas the peaks near 1395 cm−1, 1563 cm−1, 1666 cm−1 and 1081 cm−1 correspond to the tensile vibrations of CN, CC, CO, and CO, indicating that there are many polar functional groups on the surface of the CDs (Fig. 4a). Located at 586 cm−1, the adsorption peak reflected the FeO stretching vibration of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The peak at 947 cm−1 was attributed mainly to the absorption of Si–O–Fe, and the peak at 1097 cm−1 was the stretching vibration peak of Si–O–Si produced by the hydrolysis of TEOS. There was a characteristic waveform of CC at 1639 cm−1 and an unsaturated CH stretching vibration peak at 2957 cm−1. The absorption peak at 1726 cm−1 was the CO stretching vibration peak at KH570. In addition, owing to the presence of many CO bonds in MAA and EGDMA, the absorption intensity is significantly enhanced at 1726 cm−1. The intensity of the FeO characteristic peak decreased because of the encapsulation of the imprinting layer (Fig. 4b). The above results prove that the molecular imprinting layer synthesis of Fe3O4 and DES–MMIP is successful.

The elemental composition of the surface of the CDs was determined via XPS (Fig. S3). The whole pattern shows that the main peaks of the CDs are 532.04 eV, 401.08 eV, and 284.96 eV, which are the characteristic peaks of O1s, N1s, and C1s, respectively. The relative element contents were 40.9 %, 13.8 % and 45.3 %, respectively, indicating the successful doping of nitrogen atoms into the CDs. In fine–structure spectroscopy, the types of carbon included C–C/C=C (284.8 eV), CN (285.98 eV) and C=N/C=O (288.58 eV); the types of nitrogen included pyridine nitrogen (399.98 eV) and graphite nitrogen (401.78 eV); and the types of oxygen included CO (531.74 eV) and CO (533.14 eV). Overall, these results indicated that N was successfully doped into the synthesized CDs and that –COOH and –NH2 were abundant on the surface of the CDs.

Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2 and DES–MMIP were analyzed via XRD. The six characteristic diffraction peaks of Fe3O4 appeared at 2θ = 30.05°, 35.4°, 43.2°, 53.6°, 57.1°, and 62.8°, which were essentially consistent with the standard data of Fe3O4, corresponding to the six crystal facets of Fe3O4 ((220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440)) (Fig. 5a). Strong diffraction peaks can be observed at the same position for Fe3O4@SiO2 and DES–MMIP, indicating that Fe3O4 did not change its diffraction peak position after surface modification. These magnetic particles are particles with Fe3O4 as the core.

The relationship between the weight and temperature changes of materials was studied via TGA to evaluate their thermal durability (Fig. 5b). As seen from the TG curves, the thermal stability of the materials was good below 200 °C. With increasing temperature, there was no significant change in the quality of the materials, and the weight of the Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2 and DES–MMIP decreased by 4.48 %, 3.29 %, and 5.64 %, respectively. When further heated to 800 °C, the weight losses of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@SiO2 were 8.11 % and 4.02 %, respectively, while DES–MMIP underwent significant thermal decomposition, and the weight loss reached 77.03 %. It is possible that the degradation of the imprinting layer was promoted by high temperature, indicating successful imprinting.

The hysteresis loop was assessed using VSM to evaluate the magnetic properties of the materials (Fig. 5c). The materials clearly exhibited a centrosymmetric state, with almost zero coercive force and residual magnetization, indicating that they possessed superparamagnetism. With an external magnetic field, DES–MMIP can be rapidly isolated from the adsorption solution. The saturation magnetizations of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2–CH=CH2 and DES–MMIP are 75.8 emu/g, 63.3 emu/g and 32.2 emu/g, respectively. This is because nonmagnetic surface modifiers and polymers are coated on the magnetic core surface, resulting in a decrease in the strength of the synthesized material as the shell structure increases. However, the magnetization intensity of DES–MMIP was still high enough to achieve rapid separation from the adsorbent solution under the guidance of an external magnet, and it can be applied in actual sample separation analysis.

The photoluminescence spectra of the CDs were measured to further investigate their optical properties (Fig. 6a). The results revealed that the fluorescence emission peak intensity of the CDs shifted in the longer wavelength direction with increasing excitation wavelength, and the emission peak redshifted, indicating that the excitation wavelength of the CDs was dependent. Further research was conducted on the pH correlation of the CDs, and it was found that as the pH of the solution increased, the absorption peak intensity first increased and then decreased, reaching its maximum value at pH 10 (Fig. 6b). In addition, the performance of the CDs was further investigated through stability analysis. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs was almost constant within 12 min (Fig. 6c), while there was a slight change within 30 days (Fig. 6d), indicating that the CDs had excellent stability.

The dried CDs were redispersed in ultrapure water, resulting in a brown–yellow color under natural light and a bright blue color under a 365 nm UV lamp. To elucidate the quenching mechanism of CDs by DOX, we investigated the UV–Vis spectra of the CDs, DOX, and CDs + DOX mixtures (Fig. 7a). Compared with the absorption spectra of the CDs and DOX, the spectrum of the mixture did not exhibit new absorption peaks, indicating that the interaction between the CDs and DOX did not produce new compounds. Static quenching can lead to a change in the UV–Vis spectral absorption peak of the CDs, which is attributed to the fact that static quenching can cause the production of nonfluorescent ground–state complexes (John et al., 2022), thus excluding the effect of static quenching. To further clarify the induction mechanism, the absorption spectra of DOX and the fluorescence spectra of the CDs were studied (Fig. 7b). The results revealed that the absorption spectrum of DOX overlapped with the excitation or emission spectra of the CDs, indicating that the fluorescence of the CDs quenched by DOX may be attributed to FRET or IFE (Liu et al., 2019). After that, the fluorescence quenching mechanism was further elucidated by measuring the fluorescence attenuation curve. If the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs changes significantly after the addition of inhibitors, it is attributed to FRET. In contrast, it can be considered a contribution of IFE (Zu et al., 2017). Compared with the fluorescence lifetime (2.28 ns) of DOX, the fluorescence lifetime (2.50 ns) after the addition of CDs did not significantly change, indicating that the quenching mechanism may be induced by the IFE (Fig. 7c).

The application of DES–MMIP under optimal conditions can achieve the best enrichment efficiency. Therefore, the factors affecting MSPE, such as the amount of adsorbent, adsorption time, eluent type, elution volume, elution time and sample volume, were investigated. All the optimization experiments were conducted in triplicate via the single factor method.

The amount of magnetic materials used affects the extraction efficiency to a great extent. On the one hand, insufficient adsorbents can lead to incomplete adsorption, and on the other hand, although excessive adsorbents can achieve complete adsorption, they increase the volume of the eluent, causing unnecessary waste. Within the range of 10–60 mg, the recovery of DOX first increased and then stabilized, and the adsorption capacity reached the maximum value at 45 mg, indicating that the optimal adsorption dosage sufficient to enrich DOX in the sample was 45 mg (Fig. S4a). This is because the number of available active sites increases with increasing dosage of adsorbent. After reaching adsorption and desorption equilibrium, the recovery rate of DOX did not continue to improve with increasing adsorbent dosage, suggesting that the optimal adsorption dosage for DOX enrichment was 45 mg. In MSPE, an appropriate adsorption time is necessary to achieve adsorption equilibrium and maximize efficiency. When the adsorption time is too short, resulting in incomplete adsorption behavior, whereas when the time is too long, the adsorption efficiency no longer fluctuates significantly after reaching equilibrium. Within 20–90 min, the recovery rate of DOX gradually increased and reached its maximum value at 90 min (Fig. S4b). The target analyte may fully interact with the adsorbent as time increases. When surface adsorption and desorption reach equilibrium, it can be considered that time is not a factor affecting adsorption efficiency. Therefore, the adsorption time was selected as 90 min. Whether the target analyte can be effectively eluted from the adsorbent material is an important factor affecting its detection recovery rate. The elution efficiency of MeOH, ethanol, ACN, n–hexane, and MeOH/AA solutions (9:1, v:v) for DOX was investigated. The mixed solution of MeOH/AA has the best elution effect, followed by MeOH and ethanol, and the elution effect of n–hexane is the worst (Fig. S4c). This is attributed to the high polarity of MeOH, which easily reacts with polymers to form strong hydrogen bonds, and the addition of AA can enhance the solvent's elution effect. The volume of the eluent is one of the important factors affecting the recovery rate of the target analyte on the adsorbent. As the elution volume increased, the recovery rate of DOX gradually increased and reached its maximum at 10 mL. After that, the volume of the eluent continued to increase, and the recovery rate remained essentially unchanged (Fig. S4d). The desorption time is also a key factor affecting the desorption effect, so it is necessary to determine the desorption time. As the elution time increased, the extraction efficiency of DOX gradually improved. After 12 min, increasing the elution time did not result in significant changes in extraction efficiency (Fig. S4e). The sample volume affects the enrichment factor of the target substance (Fig. S4f). As the sample volume increased, the recovery rate of DOX gradually increased, reaching its maximum value at 40 mL. With increasing sample volume, the recovery rate actually decreased; this is because when the volume is too large, the limited adsorbent cannot adsorb more target substances. Therefore, 40 mL was chosen as the optimal sample volume for the adsorption process. Doxycycline is an amphoteric compound, rich in hydroxyl and amine groups in its structure, and its interaction with adsorbent is susceptible to pH changes. Therefore, pH is one of the key factors in the MSPE process. The effects of pH on DOX enrichment were investigated in the range of 2–12 (Fig. S4g). The results showed that the pH condition with the best enrichment performance was pH = 5. The recovery rate of DOX at other pH values was lower than that at pH = 5, which may be attributed to the fact that DOX has a pKa of 3.0 and is easily destroyed under alkaline conditions (Xu et al., 2021), resulting in a low recovery rate. In addition, an increase in pH value will affect the charge of DOX, inhibiting the π–π interaction between DOX and the adsorbent (Gao et al., 2012). Therefore, the optimal pH was chosen to be 5.

To clarify the rate control and mass transfer mechanism of the adsorption process, we investigated the variation in the adsorption capacity of DES–MMIP (DES–MNIP) for the target analyte with adsorption time (Fig. S5). The amount of DOX adsorbed increased rapidly within 60 min, followed by a slow increase in the adsorption rate until reaching equilibrium at 90 min; this occurred because there were many DOX adsorption sites on the surface of DES–MMIP, which can rapidly integrate with DOX. Over time, the imprinted holes continued to be occupied, and the number of binding sites decreased. The adsorption tended to be saturated, resulting in a slow increase in the adsorption capacity. The adsorption behavior of DES–MNIP was similar to that of DES–MMIP, but the adsorption capacity of the former was significantly lower than that of the latter; this may be because DES–MNIP lacks ordered imprinting cavities and cannot specifically bind to DOX, resulting in a lower adsorption capacity.

The pseudo–first–order model and pseudo–second–order model were used to evaluate the dynamic behavior of DES–MMIP (DES–MNIP). The equations were as follows (Uddin et al., 2017):

where Qt (μg·g−1) and Qe (μg·g−1) are the adsorption capacity at t (min) and equilibrium, respectively. And k1 and k2 represent adsorption constants, corresponding to the pseudo–first–order adsorption model and pseudo–second–order adsorption model, respectively.

Compared with that of the pseudo–first–order model, the correlation coefficient of the pseudo–second–order kinetic model (R2 = 0.99705) is closer to 1, indicating better linear fit, and the Qe value is consistent with the experimental results (Table 1 ). Therefore, the adsorption process of DES–MMIP (DES–MNIP) conforms to a pseudo–second–order model, indicating that the adsorption process is mainly chemical bonding accompanied by physical adsorption, that the adsorption process is rate controlled (Kalinke et al., 2016), and that the prepared DES–MMIP–imprinted adsorbents have good kinetic properties.

The adsorption capacity of DES–MMIP (DES–MNIP) for DOX was determined in the concentration range of 10–250 μg·L−1 to evaluate the binding performance of the materials. The adsorption capacity of DES–MMIP (DES–MNIP) increased with increasing DOX concentration. Compared with that of DES–MNIP, the adsorption capacity of DES–MMIP for DOX significantly increased, indicating that DES–MMIP had selective adsorption, strong recognition ability and a large adsorption capacity for DOX. To more intuitively illuminate the difference in the adsorption performance of DES–MMIP (DES–MNIP) and further clarify the adsorption mechanism, the Langmuir, Freundlich and Temkin adsorption isotherm models were adopted to analyze the adsorption data. The Langmuir isotherm model assumes that the surface adsorption sites of the adsorbent are uniformly distributed and that the adsorption process is single–layer adsorption (Gholipour & Mofarahi, 2016). The Freundlich isothermal model assumes that the adsorbent is a multimolecular layer with uneven adsorption sites on the adsorbent surface (Lu et al., 2019). The Temkin isotherm model assumes that the adsorption heat varies linearly with temperature (Pursell et al., 2011). The equations were as follows:

where Ce(μg·L−1) is the adsorption equilibrium concentration and where Qe(μg·g−1) and Qmax(μg·g−1) are the equilibrium and saturation adsorption capacities of DES–MMIP (DES–MNIP), respectively. KL, KF, and B are Langmuir, Freundlich, and Temkin constants, respectively. A represents the adsorption binding constant, and n is the empirical constant (a value of 1/n less than 1.0 indicates that the template molecule is easy to adsorb).

From the isothermal adsorption model results (Fig. 8 ) and the fitting parameters and fitting degree R2 values (Table S3), the fitting degree of the Langmuir model (R2 = 0.9911, 0.9952) was more ideal compared with those of the Freundlich and Temkin models, indicating that the Langmuir equation was suitable for simulating the adsorption of DES–MMIP (DES–MNIP). The saturated adsorption capacity of DES–MMIP (221.20 μg·g−1) was much greater than that of DES–MNIP (117.35 μg·g−1), indicating that the imprinting effect of this imprinted polymer is excellent and can significantly enhance the adsorption capacity of the target substance. On the basis of the theoretical assumption of the Langmuir isotherm model and the fact that chemical adsorption is generally single–layer adsorption, the adsorption process can be preliminarily judged as a chemisorption process.

Generally, other antibiotics in the actual food samples may affect the detection of DOX. To investigate the specificity of the method for DOX, the selectivity of DES–MMIP (DES–MNIP) for OTC, CTC, MC, TC and DOX was studied under optimal conditions (Fig. 9a). The recovery rate of DES–MMIP for DOX adsorption is much greater than that of DES–MNIP, which may be attributed to the presence of many specific recognition sites for DOX in the imprinting layer. However, DES–MNIP has a low degree of enrichment and nonselectivity in adsorbing structural analogs, and the recovery amount is always lower than that of DES–MMIP, indicating that DES–MMIP has more surface imprinting sites for recognizing DOX (Fig. 9b). The imprinting factor αDOX (2.3714) was greater than αother (Table S4), which indicated that DES-MMIP could be used to selectively separate DOX from the mixture solution.

The initial adsorption capacity was set to 100 % as a reference, and the adsorption rates of the six adsorption desorption cycles were 97.54 %, 96.13 %, 94.82 %, and 91.67 %, 89.26 %, 87.98 %, respectively (Fig. S6). The adsorption rate of the adsorbent decreased with increasing number of adsorption cycles. However, during the seventh adsorption, the adsorption rate was still as high as 87.98 %, which is attributed to the relatively stable spatial structure of DES–MMIP, the relatively strong binding sites, and the stable imprinted cavity structure under acidic conditions.

To obtain the optimal fluorescence detection parameters, important parameters such as the CDs concentration, solution pH, and incubation time were optimized. When the concentration of CDs is too low, the amount of CDs is insufficient to interact with the target substance; in contrast, self–quenching may occur. When the CDs concentration was <75 μg·mL−1, the ΔF value increased with increasing CDs concentration, and when the CDs concentration was greater than 75 μg·mL−1, the ΔF value began to decrease (Fig. 10a). Therefore, the optimal CDs concentration is 75 μg·mL−1. The properties of the carboxyl and amino groups on the surface are affected by the pH of the solution, thereby affecting the fluorescence response of the CDs. The results in Fig. 10b indicate that as the pH increased, the fluorescence quenching effect gradually strengthened. When the pH was 10, ΔF decreased most significantly, while the pH continued to increase, and the fluorescence quenching effect weakened. In addition, we investigated the fluorescence response time of the CDs to DOX. With increasing incubation time, ΔF decreased slightly at 0–4 min and reached a stable level at 4 min, with no significant change, indicating that the response time of the interaction of DOX with the CDs was fast and that the optimal incubation time was 4 min (Fig. 10c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method analytical performance and comparative study
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal optimization conditions, the linear range, detection limit and quantification limit were investigated. The ΔF value had a good linear relationship within 0.5–50 μg·L−1 (R2 = 0.9936) (Fig. S7). The limit of detection and limit of quantification were 0.34 nM and 1.13 nM, respectively, which are lower than the limit of detection in the literature (Li et al., 2023), indicating that this method has high sensitivity for DOX detection and can be applied to the determination of low concentrations of DOX in actual samples. To verify the stability and reliability of the method, intraday and interday precision were conducted separately. The results revealed that the intraday and interday precisions of the DOX concentration were 3.41 %, 2.31 %, and 1.12 % and 4.37 %, 3.15 %, and 1.92 %, respectively.

In recent years, many studies have reported the application of fluorescence spectroscopy in the analysis and detection of DOX. Li et al. synthesized fluorescent CDs via a hydrothermal method, coupled them with thermosensitive magnetic MIPs for the enrichment and detection of DOX. Zhang et al. synthesized copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) via chemical reduction technology and developed a fluorescent nanoprobe for DOX detection (Zhang et al., 2024). Feng et al. synthesized N–CDs using citric acid and ethylenediamine and bound them to DOX–imprinted polymers (Feng et al., 2018). Han et al. assembled histidine and serine functionalized graphene quantum dots (His–GQDs–Ser) into a luminescent metal–organic framework (MOF) to fabricate a unique fluorescent nanoprobe that recognizes DOX (Han et al., 2023). Raut et al. developed blue luminescent N–CQDs via a microwave–assisted method (Raut et al., 2022). By comparing the sensing materials used in Table 2 , the MIP synthesized in this study has two obvious advantages. On the one hand, fluorescent carbon dot material was used to achieve rapid separation by sensing changes in the external magnetic field, avoiding tedious operations such as centrifugation and supernatant extraction during the extraction process. The fluorescence detection method greatly improved the detection sensitivity with a detection limit of 0.34 nM. Another advantage is using ChCl–MAA DES system as a functional monomer, not only enhancing the adsorption capacity of imprinting products and reusable times, more importantly, this study conforms to the green concept of environmental protection. It provided a new material and research approach for efficient analysis of DOX in complex matrices. Notably, the adsorption capacity of DES-MMIP prepared in this study is significantly improved, which can provide a data support for trace detection of other types of antibiotics such as quinolones and macrolides.

Generally, there may be antibiotic residues in commercially available meat, eggs, milk and other foods, which pose a certain threat to the environment and human health. Therefore, a sensitive, efficient, and highly selective method for the enrichment and detection of trace substances is urgently needed. We validated the proposed method with samples of livestock, poultry, and seafood products, including pork, beef, milk, eggs, and fish. To further evaluate the feasibility of the proposed method for extracting and quantifying DOX, the samples were preprocessed, and DOX solutions with concentrations of 1 μg·Kg−1, 5 μg·Kg−1, and 10 μg·Kg−1 were added, followed by analysis of the blank and spiked samples (n = 3). Under the optimal detection conditions, no DOX was detected in the samples, and the fortified recovers ranged from 87.5 %–97.8 % (Table 3 ), which was slightly higher than that of HPLC–UV (Fig. S8, Table S5). This is because one of the advantages of fluorescence detection method is high sensitivity, and it eliminates interference, selective enrichment of the target, and the adsorbent can adsorb more target substances. Therefore, the sample solution can eliminate the tedious sample pretreatment process and obtain excellent detection results when using the established method for determination. The above results indicated that the method developed in this study combined rapid pretreatment, fluorescent CDs and MIT, which has high accuracy and practicality in the detection of DOX in food samples, and meets the requirements of quantitative detection of DOX in food samples. This simple and time–saving reprocessing method can be used as a supplementary method to detect conventional veterinary drug residue, providing new ideas for food safety testing and market supervision. In the future, a rapid detection kit for tetracycline antibiotics in food may be developed successfully by preprocessing the food and putting it into the kit. The values can be read, making it easier for the general public to monitor illegal additives.

However, although DES–MMIP has shown significant advantages in overcoming the problems of traditional MIPs, there are still some limitations in practical applications. MIPs exhibit good properties in organic solvents, most of the interactions between substances occur in water rather than in organic systems. Therefore, imprinting in water systems remains a challenge. For this purpose, more biocompatible and hydrophilic functional monomers can be selected to reduce the impact of hydrogen bonding. This can not only improve the stability of MIPs in water systems, but also enhance their ability to recognize target molecules. What's more, according to the structure of MIPs, developing and modifying a wider range of core materials to encapsulate MIPs shells is an important development direction. Therefore, a combination of computer simulation, response surface, and characterization techniques can be used to control the thickness of the MIPs imprinting layers and obtain sufficient recognition sites. This can not only improve the recognition performance of MIPs, but also enhance their stability and durability in complex environments. Furthermore, selecting appropriate synthesis methods can overcome these issues. The suspension and emulsion polymerization methods can directly obtain uniform MIP particles with high adsorption capacity and speed. These methods enable template molecules to be buried deeper and more tightly, thereby improving the recognition ability and stability of MIPs. However, both methods may also lead to difficult elution progress, and researchers need to carefully control the reaction conditions during the synthesis process to ensure the quality of the final product. In general, MIPs have broad application prospects in modern technology. For example, in environmental monitoring, MIPs can be used to detect trace pollutants in water, thereby protecting the ecological environment and human health (Wang et al., 2022). In medical diagnosis, MIPs can be used for early disease detection, improving the accuracy and timeliness of diagnosis (Li & Liu, 2024). In the field of food safety, MIPs can be used to detect harmful substances in food to ensure the safety of food (Zhou et al., 2024).

In summary, this experiment simulated the molecular dynamics process of DES in a composite system and investigated the synthesis, characterization, and adsorption behavior of novel DES–based magnetic MIPs that specifically recognized DOX. Coupled with fluorescent carbon dots, it was successfully applied in the selective enrichment of DOX. The results suggested that, compared with DES–MNIP, DES–MMIP has excellent selectivity and adsorption efficiency and conforms to the pseudo–second–order kinetic model and Langmuir isothermal adsorption model. In addition, the synthesized CDs have good water solubility and fluorescence stability. On the basis of the study of the quenching mechanism, it can be concluded that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs by DOX contributes to the IFE. Finally, the established assay has good methodological parameters and has been successfully applied to the quantitative determination of DOX in foodborne foods. Overall, the synthesized DES–MMIP and the established detection method are expected to serve as new alternatives for the specific separation and enrichment of DOX in complex sample matrices, with important reference significance for the sustainable production of safe food, ecological environment management, and human health protection.
